message.text: Remote Code Execution via eval() on user-supplied input. The endpoint POST /api/eval/python passes the user-provided 'code' field from JSON request body directly to Python's eval() built-in: `result = eval(code)`. This grants any unauthenticated remote attacker full Python runtime access, enabling arbitrary command execution (e.g., `__import__('os').system('rm -rf /')`), file system access, environment variable exfiltration, and reverse shell establishment. eval() must never be used on untrusted input. Remove this endpoint entirely or replace with a sandboxed execution environment (e.g., RestrictedPython, subprocess with seccomp, or a container-isolated runner).

The POST /api/eval/python endpoint executes arbitrary Python code but requires no authentication. Any network-reachable client can invoke code execution on the server. This is a critical access control failure (CWE-306). All code execution endpoints must require strong authentication (e.g., API key, OAuth token, or session-based auth) and should be restricted to explicitly authorized users or roles.

No authorization checks on the code execution endpoint. Even if authentication were added, there is no role-based or permission-based gate to restrict who can execute arbitrary Python code. A code execution capability should be limited to administrator or operator roles at minimum, with audit logging of every invocation.

No input validation, sanitization, or length restriction on the 'code' parameter before it is passed to eval(). There is no allowlist of permitted operations, no AST-level inspection, and no timeout or resource limit. Even in a legitimate code-evaluation use case, the absence of any input constraints allows denial-of-service via infinite loops (`while True: pass`), memory exhaustion, or CPU-intensive computations.
